h1: Free Fall Distance Calculator by Time
description: Calculate how far an object falls under gravity. Free instant tool for physics problems, students, and engineers.
intro: Physical laws are universal and deterministic. Understanding free fall calculations helps you solve real-world motion, energy, force, and physics problems. This free calculator computes the distance an object falls in time t under gravity. Accurate and updated for 2026.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **h = ½·g·t²**. Enter your time and gravity values to instantly calculate free fall distance. See how far an object falls under gravity in seconds.
useCases: Technical calculations for free fall physics problems
Teaching and learning free fall mechanics

explanation:
**Applied physics** solves real-world problems: from free fall to household electricity. These formulas have been validated through centuries of experimentation.

## How It Works

This calculator applies the free fall equation directly to your inputs. It validates typical ranges and alerts you to unusual values that might indicate an error.

## Important Notes

These results are for reference only. For critical decisions (safety, structural design, legal matters), consult with a qualified engineer or specialist. Formulas are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: Does this calculator account for air resistance?
faq.a: No. This assumes ideal free fall (no air resistance). With air, the physics is more complex (differential equations). For dense objects falling short distances, the difference is minimal.

faq.q: How do I calculate fall time from a known height?
faq.a: Reverse the formula: t = √(2h/g). Example: from 20 meters → t = √(40/9.81) ≈ 2.02 seconds.

faq.q: Can I use this for calculating jumps?
faq.a: Yes, but only for the first second or two of free fall. After that, air resistance becomes important and real-world jumps won't match this formula.

faq.q: What about gravity on other planets?
faq.a: Gravity varies everywhere. Moon: 1.62 m/s², Mars: 3.71 m/s², Jupiter: 24.79 m/s². Change the g value to explore free fall on different worlds.

faq.q: From what height is a fall dangerous?
faq.a: Falls above 50 feet (15 m) pose serious injury risk. That's roughly 1.75 seconds of free fall, reaching about 55 mph. Higher falls are life-threatening.
